course_description:
Reinforced concrete theory for flexure, shear, compression; strength and serviceability limit states; slabs - lower bound analysis. Strength and deformation properties; material selection; mix design and specification; durability; code requirements; case studies; low cementitious binders; laboratory.

Concrete is the most widely used construction material in the world. Buildings, bridges, dams and roads – the infrastructure our cities and communities rely on– use reinforced concrete as the design material of choice.

In this course, you will learn how to design key elements in reinforced concrete structures – beams, slabs and columns. You will also learn about concrete as a material, and how as an engineer the choices you make can lead to more durable and sustainable concrete structures.

The course content provides the foundation on which advanced reinforced and prestressed concrete design skills are developed in later semesters.

course_aims:
The aim of the Reinforced Concrete Structures part of this course is:

The purpose of the Concrete Technology component of this course is:

The skills learnt in both the Concrete Technology and Reinforced Concrete Design sections of this course are used in numerous design and analysis courses in later semesters.

learning_outcomes.description: In-Depth Knowledge - Beams: The student will be able to analyse and design a reinforced concrete beam for flexure/bending and shear. The student will be able to analyse a reinforced concrete beam for serviceability response and calculate and evaluate deflections and crack control in accordance with the relevant code, AS3600. The student will be able to detail the reinforcement of a beam to comply with the regulations set out in AS3600.

In-Depth Knowledge - Columns: The student will be able to analyse and design both slender and stocky reinforced concrete columns. The student will be able to analyse the biaxial bending behaviour of a column in accordance with the relevant code, AS3600. The student will be able to detail the reinforcement of a column to comply with the regulations set out in AS3600.

In-Depth Knowledge - Slabs: The student will be able to analyse and design a reinforced concrete slab. The students will be able to calculate design bending moments and shears using codified analysis techniques and the Hillerborg Strip method. The student will be able to analyse a reinforced concrete slab for serviceability response and calculate and evaluate deflections and crack control in accordance with the relevant code, AS3600. The student will be able to detail the reinforcement of a slab to comply with the regulations set out in AS3600.

Technical Knowledge (Concrete Technology): The student will be able to identify and describe the fundamental properties of concrete. The students will be able to specify the appropriate types of concrete for a range of environments. They will understand and be able to describe how the technology of the concrete and short and long term performance is related.
Hands-on skills - The laboratory classes will provide students with the opportunity to develop practical hands-on knowledge of working with concrete and reinforcing steel.
Critical Thinking: The student will be able to apply analysis and design skills to unfamiliar structural elements and applications.
Communication: The student will demonstrate an ability to effectively communicate the results of engineering analysis and design to other engineers or professionals.

Via Blackboard - Refer to instructions on Blackboard for submission via Gradescope.
Non-compliant submissions will receive zero marks
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Solutions will be released 7 days after submission to aid in study and student progression.
Students with a valid reason for a longer extension will have the weighting of the remaining submissions increased on a pro-rated basis.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Concrete Practical
assessment_details.due_date: Week 4 - Week 13 Group will complete all report requirements (calculations etc) during the prac classes. Students should consult their personal timetable to confirm the TWO dates they are required to attend the prac classes.
assessment_details.task_description: Students must attend 2 practical classes. Students will undertake calculations as a group during the prac class. To access the concrete prac classes it is a mandatory requirement that you have Completed the U/G Student Lab Safety Induction Assessment (UGRD01) prior to attending a practical experiment session. Students only have to complete the assessment once during their undergraduate degree. (by Friday of week 3) Steel capped safety boots & Safety glasses Your student ID card If you do not have all of these items at the time of your first prac, you will NOT be permitted to access the laboratory. Suitable clothing is as follows: lab coats are NOT required students must wear shirts with sleeves (no singlet tops) and suitable shorts/pants. Very short shorts are not permitted - shorts should come to the knee. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Group will complete all calculations during the prac classes.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student us unable to attend their designated practical class they must apply for an extension and will be allocated to another practical class where possible.
If there is no alternative practical class available, a student with an approved extension will be provided with alternative assessment via a virtual practical class.
Students should be aware that Prac 1 runs Week 4 to Week Week 9 and Prac 2 runs Week 8 to week 13.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
As time is provided within the practical class to complete all work, no late submissions will be accepted.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (28 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture (Reinforced Concrete) During lectures key concepts in reinforced concrete design are introduced. Examples of the design principles being developed are given. The lectures signpost the way for students to learn in the workshops, practical sessions and their own study. Discussion and questions are encouraged. A detailed outline of lecture topics is available on the BlackBoard site. Reinforced Concrete Lectures will be in person Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (28 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Workshop In the workshop sessions the students can apply the techniques presented in lectures to solve problems. The workshops provide the students with the opportunity to discuss any questions which arise from the lectures and the practical sessions with the tutor, teaching staff and amongst themselves. The independent completion of workshop is the single most significant learning activity students will undertake for the reinforced concrete design component. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (28 Jul - 02 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Reading - Reinforced Concrete Design Students will spend time outside the designated contact hours reviewing lecture material, working on workshop problems and reading reference texts relevant to the Reinforced Concrete Design section of the course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 5 (28 Jul - 31 Aug)
learning_activities.topic: Pre-recorded Lecture (Concrete Technology) During the pre-recorded lectures key concepts in concrete technology are introduced. The lectures signpost the way for students to learn in the workshops, practical sessions and their own study. Discussion and questions are encouraged. A detailed outline of lecture topics is available on the BlackBoard site. Concrete Technology Lectures will be pre-recorded and there will be an in-person Lecture used for review and discussion. See the Schedule on BlackBoard. Learning outcomes: L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 5 (28 Jul - 31 Aug)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture Review (Concrete Technology) During the CT Review lecture the lecturer will lead a discussion and Q&A session on the relevant CT lectures.. Discussion and questions are encouraged. A detailed outline of lecture topics is available on the BlackBoard site. Concrete Technology Lectures will be pre-recorded and there will be an in-person Lecture used for review and discussion. See the Schedule on BlackBoard. Learning outcomes: L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 5 (28 Jul - 31 Aug)
learning_activities.topic: Reading - Concrete Technology Students will spend time outside the designated contact hours reviewing lecture material, working on workshop problems and reading reference texts relevant to the Concrete Technology section of the course. Additional reading is essential if students want to pass the concrete technology component. Learning outcomes: L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 13
learning_activities.topic: Prac Classes Students will attend two practical sessions as per their personal timetable. In these sessions the students will work in a group of approximately 12 -14 students to design, construct and test 2 reinforced concrete beams. The group will be responsible for analysing the aggregates; designing the concrete mix; designing and constructing the reinforcing cage; mixing and placing the concrete; and testing the beams to failure. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
